title: Sekhmet: Anger as Divine Force
dek: Your fury is not a flaw in your character. It is the oldest medicine in the world, moving through your fascia and demanding that you finally stand your ground. When the jaw tightens and the heat rises, you are not failing at peace - you are remembering the divine force of Sekhmet, the lioness who knows exactly what must be destroyed so that you can live.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Heat in the Jaw

You wake up at three in the morning, and the heat is already there. It does not start in your mind. It starts in the masseter muscles, the thick bands of tissue at the hinge of your jaw, clamping down so hard your teeth ache. It moves down the sides of your neck, into the levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears as if you are bracing for a blow. Your chest feels tight, the diaphragm locked, refusing to take in a full breath. You lie there in the dark, the sheets tangled around your legs, and you think: *I am failing again. I am not peaceful. I am not spiritual. I am just angry.*

But this is not pathology. This is biology. This is the body remembering what the mind has tried to forget. You have been taught that anger is ugly, that it is a moral failure, a sign that you are not evolved enough, not calm enough, not good enough. You have been told to breathe through it, to meditate it away, to send love and light to the people who have crossed your boundaries. But the body knows better. The body knows that when the heat rises, it is not a mistake. It is a messenger.

In the ancient Egyptian pantheon, there is a goddess named Sekhmet. She is depicted as a woman with the head of a lioness, crowned with the sun disk and the uraeus serpent. Her name means "She Who Is Powerful." She is the goddess of war, of destruction, of plague - but she is also the goddess of healing. The ancient physicians were her priests. They understood something that we have forgotten: that the force that destroys is the same force that heals. You cannot have one without the other. When the infection is deep, you need the fever to burn it out. When the boundary has been breached, you need the anger to rebuild the wall.

Your anger is Sekhmet moving through your veins. It is the divine force of protection, rising up to say: *No more. This stops here.*

## The Anatomy of Fury

When you feel that sudden surge of rage, what is actually happening in your body? The sympathetic nervous system kicks into gear. The adrenal glands, sitting like little hats on top of your kidneys, dump cortisol and adrenaline into your bloodstream. Your heart rate spikes. Blood is shunted away from your digestive organs and toward your extremities - your hands, your legs - preparing you to fight or flee. The psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs, tightens, readying you to spring.

This is a brilliant, ancient mechanism. It is designed to keep you alive. But in our modern world, we are rarely fighting off physical predators. We are fighting off passive-aggressive emails, dismissive doctors, partners who won't listen, systems that crush us. The threat is chronic, and so the anger becomes chronic. The psoas stays tight. The jaw stays clamped. The liver, which in Traditional Chinese Medicine is the organ associated with anger and the smooth flow of qi, becomes stagnant. You feel stuck, irritable, exhausted.

Gabor Maté, the physician and author who has spent his life studying the connection between stress and disease, writes extensively about what happens when we suppress this vital force. He notes that the repression of anger is a major risk factor for autoimmune diseases, chronic pain, and cancer. When you swallow your fury, it doesn't disappear. It turns inward. The immune system, confused by the suppressed energy, begins to attack the body itself. Maté teaches that healthy anger is a boundary defense. It is the immune system of the psyche. When you lose your capacity to say no, your body will eventually say it for you, often in the form of illness.

You are not doing yourself any favors by pretending to be fine. You are not protecting anyone by swallowing the fire. You are only burning yourself down from the inside.

## The Lioness in the Room

Think about a time when you were truly, deeply angry. Not just annoyed, but enraged. Where did you feel it? Did your hands shake? Did your vision narrow? Did you feel a sudden, terrifying clarity?

That clarity is the gift of Sekhmet. When the lioness is awake, there is no room for confusion. There is no room for self-doubt. The anger cuts through the fog of socialization, the endless conditioning that tells you to be nice, to be accommodating, to make yourself small. Anger says: *This is what is true. This is what is unacceptable.*

Karla McLaren, an empathy researcher and author of *The Language of Emotions*, calls anger the "honorable sentry." She teaches that anger's primary job is to protect your boundaries and your sense of self. When anger arises, it is asking a specific question: *What must be protected, and what must be restored?* McLaren suggests that instead of trying to calm down, we should ask our anger what it needs. We should welcome the sentry, thank it for its vigilance, and listen to its report.

But listening to anger is terrifying, especially for women. We have been punished for our anger since we were little girls. We have been called crazy, hysterical, difficult, shrill. We have watched what happens to women who raise their voices. So we learn to channel the anger into safer avenues. We turn it into anxiety. We turn it into depression. We turn it into perfectionism, cleaning the kitchen at midnight with a furious, tight-lipped precision. We turn it into self-hatred.

It is time to stop turning it inward. It is time to let the lioness out of the cage.

## The Medicine of the Bitter Root

In herbalism, we work with the energetics of plants. When there is stagnation in the liver, when the anger is trapped and turning into resentment, we use bitter herbs. Bitters stimulate the flow of bile, encouraging the liver to process and release what it has been holding onto. They wake up the digestive system. They move the energy downward and outward.

Dandelion root is a classic liver tonic. It is a weed, stubborn and resilient, pushing its way through cracks in the pavement. It refuses to be eradicated. When you drink a strong decoction of roasted dandelion root, you are taking in that resilience. You are reminding your body how to process the heavy, stagnant energy of unexpressed rage.

Skullcap is another ally. It is a nervine, a plant that feeds and supports the nervous system. But unlike the heavy sedatives that knock you out, skullcap is a restorative. It is specifically indicated for the person who is exhausted but wired, the person who cannot stop thinking, the person whose muscles are twitching with unspent energy. Skullcap helps to release the tension in the jaw, the neck, the shoulders. It allows the nervous system to down-regulate without suppressing the underlying emotion.

When you work with these plants, you are not trying to fix yourself. You are not trying to make the anger go away. You are simply giving your body the support it needs to process the emotion, to let the fire burn cleanly without destroying the house. You might consider keeping some [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) in your cupboard for those mornings when the liver feels heavy and the jaw is tight. Or perhaps some [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) to brew a strong infusion when the nervous system is vibrating with unexpressed fury.

## The Sacred Destruction

Sekhmet was not a gentle goddess. In the mythology, she was sent by the sun god Ra to punish humanity for their rebellion. She went on a rampage, slaughtering people and drinking their blood. She became so consumed by her bloodlust that Ra realized she would destroy all of humanity if she wasn't stopped. He poured out thousands of jugs of beer dyed red with pomegranate juice, tricking Sekhmet into thinking it was blood. She drank it, became intoxicated, and fell asleep. When she woke, the rage had passed, and she became Hathor, the goddess of love, music, and joy.

This myth is often interpreted as a warning about the dangers of unchecked anger. But look deeper. The destruction was necessary. The old order had to be torn down. And the transformation into Hathor - into love and joy - was only possible *after* the rage had been fully expressed, fully consumed. You cannot bypass the lioness to get to the love. You have to go through the fire.

Robert Augustus Masters, a psychotherapist and author of *Spiritual Bypassing*, writes about the danger of trying to jump straight to forgiveness and peace without doing the messy, embodied work of feeling our darker emotions. He calls this "blind compassion" - a compassion that avoids conflict, that refuses to set boundaries, that prioritizes being "nice" over being real. Masters teaches that true compassion is fierce. It is willing to say no. It is willing to disrupt the status quo. It is willing to be angry.

When you feel the heat rising in your chest, do not try to bypass it. Do not immediately look for the silver lining. Do not try to forgive the person who harmed you before you have even acknowledged the harm. Let the destruction happen. Let the old, false structures of your life burn down. The relationships that require you to be small, the jobs that drain your life force, the beliefs that tell you you are unworthy - let Sekhmet tear them apart.

## The Somatic Practice of Roaring

How do we actually move this energy through the body? How do we honor the lioness without burning down our actual lives?

We have to give the body a physical outlet for the biological charge. You cannot think your way out of a sympathetic nervous system response. You have to move your way out.

Here is a practice. Find a place where you will not be interrupted. Stand with your feet wider than hip-width apart, your knees deeply bent. Feel the ground beneath your feet. Feel the solidity of the earth. Bring your awareness to your psoas, deep in your pelvis. Bring your awareness to your jaw.

Take a deep breath in through your nose, filling your belly, expanding your ribs. As you exhale, open your mouth as wide as you can, stick your tongue out, and make a sound. It doesn't have to be a loud scream. It can be a deep, guttural growl. It can be a hiss. It can be a roar. Let the sound come from your belly, not your throat. Feel the vibration in your chest. Feel the tension releasing from your jaw.

Do this three times. On the third time, let your hands form claws. Let your face contort. Do not worry about looking ugly. The divine force is not concerned with being pretty. It is concerned with being true.

After you make the sound, stand still. Close your eyes. Notice what is happening in your body. Notice the tingling in your hands. Notice the heat in your chest. Notice the space that has opened up in your jaw. You have moved the energy. You have honored the sentry. You have let the lioness speak.

## The Aftermath of the Fire

When the fire has burned through, what is left?

Often, there is grief. Anger is a secondary emotion, a protector. It rises up to defend the parts of us that are vulnerable, the parts that have been hurt. When the anger has done its job, when the boundary has been set, the protector can step back, and the underlying pain can finally be felt.

You might find yourself weeping after a surge of rage. This is normal. This is the body releasing the residual tension. The tears are the cooling rain after the forest fire. They wash away the ash and prepare the soil for new growth.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist and trauma researcher who wrote *The Body Keeps the Score*, emphasizes that trauma is not just an event that happened in the past; it is the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body. When we are traumatized, our nervous system gets stuck in a state of hyperarousal or dissociation. The anger gets trapped in the muscles, the fascia, the viscera. Van der Kolk's work shows that healing requires us to re-establish ownership of our bodies. We have to learn to tolerate our physical sensations, including the terrifying sensation of rage. We have to learn that we can feel the heat without being destroyed by it.

This is the work of a lifetime. It is not a quick fix. It is a slow, patient process of rebuilding trust with your own animal body. It is learning to listen to the whispers before they become screams. It is learning to honor the tension in your shoulders, the clenching in your gut, the sudden flash of heat in your face.

## The Daily Tending

You do not have to wait for a crisis to honor your anger. You can tend to it daily, the way you would tend to a fire in the hearth.

Pay attention to your boundaries. Notice when you say yes when you mean no. Notice the physical sensation of that betrayal - the slight sinking in the chest, the tightening of the throat. When you feel that sensation, pause. You do not have to blow up your life. You just have to acknowledge the truth. *I do not want to do this. I am angry that I feel obligated to do this.*

Support your nervous system. Drink your bitter teas. Take your nervines. A cup of [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe the digestion when the liver is working hard to process the heavy emotions. Move your body. Shake your hands. Stomp your feet. Let the energy move.

And remember Sekhmet. Remember that the force that destroys is the same force that heals. Your anger is not a flaw. It is your life force, demanding to be lived. It is the divine feminine, fierce and unapologetic, rising up to protect what is sacred.

Do not apologize for the fire. Let it burn. Let it clear the brush. Let it show you what is true.

## The Deepening of the Practice

As you continue to work with your anger, you will notice that the physical sensations begin to change. The initial, explosive heat may give way to a more sustained, grounded warmth. The tightness in the jaw may soften into a firm, resolute set of the mouth. You are moving from the acute phase of the stress response into a more integrated, embodied state of power.

This is where the real transformation happens. When you are no longer terrified of your own rage, you can begin to use it as fuel. You can channel the energy of Sekhmet into your creative work, into your activism, into the daily, necessary task of building a life that actually fits you. The anger becomes the fire in the belly that sustains you through the long, slow work of change.

Resmaa Menakem, a trauma specialist and author of *My Grandmother's Hands*, writes about the concept of "clean pain" versus "dirty pain." Clean pain is the pain that mends and can build your capacity for growth. It is the pain of setting a boundary, of having a difficult conversation, of feeling the full force of your anger and letting it move through you. Dirty pain is the pain of avoidance, of blame, of denial. It is the pain that creates more trauma. When we refuse to feel our anger, we are choosing dirty pain. We are passing the trauma on to our bodies, to our children, to our communities.

Choosing to feel the anger, choosing to let the lioness roar, is a commitment to clean pain. It is not easy. It will make you sweat. It will make your heart pound. But it will not destroy you. It will forge you.

Think of the blacksmith's fire. The metal must be heated until it is glowing red, until it is soft enough to be shaped. The hammer blows are not meant to destroy the metal; they are meant to strengthen it, to fold it over on itself until the impurities are burned away and the steel is tempered. Your anger is the fire. The challenges of your life are the hammer. You are the steel.

Do not flinch from the heat. Do not run from the anvil. Stay in the fire until the work is done.

## The Wisdom of the Fascia

We often think of emotions as happening in the brain, but the body tells a different story. The fascia, the continuous web of connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, bone, and organ in your body, is highly innervated. It is a sensory organ in its own right. And it holds memory.

When you suppress an emotion, the fascia tightens. It creates a physical holding pattern to contain the unexpressed energy. Over time, these holding patterns become chronic. The fascia thickens and hardens, restricting your movement, pulling your skeleton out of alignment. The chronic neck pain, the tight hips, the lower back ache - these are often the physical manifestations of trapped emotional energy.

To release the anger, we have to work with the fascia. We have to melt the holding patterns. This requires slow, patient, mindful movement. It requires us to bring our awareness into the dense, fibrous tissues of the body and invite them to soften.

Try this: lie on your back with your knees bent and your feet flat on the floor. Place your hands on your lower belly, just above your pubic bone. Close your eyes and take a few slow, deep breaths. Imagine that your breath is a warm, golden light, and direct that light down into your pelvis. Feel the subtle movement of the fascia under your hands as you breathe.

Now, bring to mind a situation that makes you angry. Notice what happens in your body. Does your belly tighten? Does your breath become shallow? Do your shoulders creep up toward your ears?

Instead of trying to relax, simply observe the tension. Acknowledge it. Say to your body: *I feel you. I know you are angry. It is okay.*

Stay with the sensation. Do not try to change it. Just hold it in your awareness, the way you would hold a frightened child. Often, simply acknowledging the physical reality of the emotion is enough to begin the melting process. You may feel a sudden release, a deep sigh, a softening in the belly. The fascia is letting go. The energy is moving.

This is the slow, quiet work of the lioness. She does not always have to roar. Sometimes, she just has to sit in the dark and breathe.

***

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I let myself be angry, I will never stop. I'm terrified of losing control.**
**A:** This is the most common fear, and it makes perfect biological sense. When you have suppressed a powerful force for decades, the pressure behind the dam is immense. The fear of losing control is actually the fear of the backlog. The somatic approach is not about breaking the dam all at once; it is about opening the spillway just a crack. You don't have to express the anger to the person who hurt you. You just have to let your body feel the physical sensation of it for ninety seconds. Growl in your car. Twist a towel. Let the pressure off slowly. The anger will not last forever. It is an energy wave, and like all waves, it will eventually crest and break.

**Q: How do I know the difference between healthy anger and just being toxic or abusive?**
**A:** Healthy anger is a boundary defense. It is specific, it is grounded in the present moment, and its goal is protection and restoration. It says, "Stop doing that." Toxic or abusive anger is about control, domination, and punishment. It attacks the other person's character rather than addressing the specific behavior. Somatically, healthy anger feels clear, hot, and mobilizing. Abusive anger often feels chaotic, desperate, and deeply disconnected from the body. If your anger is asking for a boundary, it is healthy. If your anger is demanding that someone else be destroyed so you can feel powerful, that is a trauma response that needs deeper tending.

**Q: I don't feel angry. I just feel tired and depressed all the time. What does that mean?**
**A:** In many cases, depression is anger turned inward. When the nervous system perceives that it is not safe to fight back or flee, it goes into a freeze response. The energy of the anger is still there, but it is trapped under a heavy blanket of dissociation and exhaustion. The jaw might still be tight, the digestion might be sluggish, but the conscious feeling of anger is inaccessible. If this is you, do not try to force the anger. Start by gently thawing the freeze. Notice small sensations in your body. Move your fingers and toes. Drink warming teas. As the nervous system begins to feel safer, the anger will naturally begin to thaw and rise to the surface. Welcome it when it does. It means you are coming back to life.

**Q: Can herbs really help with something as big as rage?**
**A:** Herbs are not a magic bullet. They will not fix your toxic workplace or heal your childhood trauma. But they are profound allies for the physical body. Anger takes a massive toll on the liver, the nervous system, and the adrenal glands. Herbs like dandelion, skullcap, and chamomile provide the physiological support your body needs to process the biochemical cascade of rage. They remind the tissues how to relax. They support the organs of elimination so the stress hormones can be cleared from the bloodstream. They are the physical scaffolding that allows you to do the emotional work.

**Q: What if my anger is directed at myself?**
**A:** Self-directed anger is often the internalized voice of the people or systems that harmed us. We learn to attack ourselves before the world can attack us; it is a twisted form of self-protection. When you notice the heat of anger turning inward, see if you can gently redirect it outward. Ask yourself: *Whose voice is this? Who taught me to hate this part of myself?* The anger belongs to the system that conditioned you, not to your own precious body. Stand up for yourself against the internalized critic. Let the lioness protect you from your own cruelest thoughts.

---
